title: unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A factory returns unique_ptr<Widget>, transferring exclusive ownership to the caller without an explicit delete. Your goal is to choose and justify an ownership representation without unnecessary shared ownership. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and initialization; Value, reference and const-reference parameters; RAII and deterministic cleanup. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: unique_ptr expresses exclusive ownership, shared_ptr expresses reference-counted shared ownership, and weak_ptr observes a shared object without extending its lifetime. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: shared ownership cycles leak, aliasing constructors and custom deleters complicate behavior, and raw pointers or references can remain valid non-owning observers. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: shared_ptr is the safest default for every pointer because more owners always prevent lifetime bugs.

includes: Prefer unique ownership and use shared ownership only when the lifetime model requires it.
Choose and justify an ownership representation without unnecessary shared ownership.
excludes: shared ownership cycles leak, aliasing constructors and custom deleters complicate behavior, and raw pointers or references can remain valid non-owning observers.

outcomes: Choose and justify an ownership representation without unnecessary shared ownership.

examples.title: unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access: defined case
examples.situation: A factory returns unique_ptr<Widget>, transferring exclusive ownership to the caller without an explicit delete.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A factory returns unique_ptr<Widget>, transferring exclusive ownership to the caller without an explicit delete.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Prefer unique_ptr for exclusive ownership, use shared_ptr only for genuine shared lifetime, and break observer cycles with weak_ptr.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Prefer unique_ptr for exclusive ownership, use shared_ptr only for genuine shared lifetime, and break observer cycles with weak_ptr.

examples.title: unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access: boundary case
examples.situation: A parent owns children strongly while each child uses weak_ptr for an optional parent link, avoiding a reference cycle.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A parent owns children strongly while each child uses weak_ptr for an optional parent link, avoiding a reference cycle.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: shared ownership cycles leak, aliasing constructors and custom deleters complicate behavior, and raw pointers or references can remain valid non-owning observers.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: shared_ptr is the safest default for every pointer because more owners always prevent lifetime bugs.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and justify an ownership representation without unnecessary shared ownership.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: shared_ptr is the safest default for every pointer because more owners always prevent lifetime bugs.
misconceptions.correction: Prefer unique_ptr for exclusive ownership, use shared_ptr only for genuine shared lifetime, and break observer cycles with weak_ptr.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: shared_ptr is the safest default for every pointer because more owners always prevent lifetime bugs.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
